zh.siteSubtitle: 唐代·李淳风 & 袁天罡
zh.homeIntro1: 推背图相传为唐朝贞观年间（约公元7世纪），唐太宗李世民命太史令李淳风与术士袁天罡推算国运而作。李淳风精通天文历法，袁天罡擅长相术占卜，两人合力推演，据说李淳风推算入神、欲罢不能，袁天罡在背后推了他一把说「天机不可再泄」，遂成书名「推背图」。
zh.homeIntro2: 全书共60象，每象由四部分组成：一个《易经》卦象、一幅寓意图画、一首四句谶语（直接预言）和一首八句颂诗（补充解说）。从第一象（唐朝开国）至第六十象（天下大同），依照历史顺序排列，每象对应一个重大历史事件或朝代更替。
zh.homeIntro3: 推背图流传千余年，版本众多，现今最广为流传的是清初文学批评家金圣叹的批注本。它被誉为中华第一预言奇书，与《烧饼歌》《马前课》并称为中国古代三大预言。然而学术界对其真伪争议颇大，部分学者认为现存版本可能经过历代增补修改，前代已验之象或为后人所加。本站整理了60象原文并进行验证分析，供读者参考。
zh.coreQuote: 「万万千千说不尽，不如推背去归休。」——推背图·第六十象
zh.kp2019: 第3象 —— 武则天称帝（已应验）
zh.kp2035: 第5象 —— 安史之乱（已应验）
zh.kp2038: 第21象 —— 靖康之变（已应验）
zh.kp2048: 第27象 —— 明太祖登极（已应验）

zh.verifySubtitle: 对已到期的预言时间点进行真实度评估
zh.footerNote: 本站内容整理自公开资料，仅供参考与讨论。推背图版本众多，现存流行版本为金圣叹批注本，其真伪及成书年代学术界尚有争议。部分「已验」之象可能为后人增补。验证评价仅代表编者观点。

en.siteSubtitle: Tang Dynasty · Li Chunfeng & Yuan Tiangang
en.homeIntro1: Tui Bei Tu (推背图, "Back-Pushing Illustration") is traditionally attributed to two Tang dynasty astrologers: Li Chunfeng, the imperial astronomer royal, and Yuan Tiangang, a renowned diviner. Legend says they were commissioned by Emperor Taizong of Tang (circa 7th century) to foretell the fate of the empire. Li Chunfeng became so absorbed in his calculations that Yuan Tiangang pushed him on the back, saying "Heaven's secrets must not be further revealed" — hence the name "Tui Bei Tu" (pushing the back).
en.homeIntro2: The complete work comprises 60 prophetic images, each consisting of four elements: an I Ching hexagram, an allegorical illustration, a four-line prophetic verse (谶, chèn), and an eight-line commentary poem (颂, sòng). Arranged in chronological order from Image 1 (founding of the Tang dynasty) to Image 60 (universal harmony), each image corresponds to a major historical event or dynastic transition in Chinese history.
en.homeIntro3: Tui Bei Tu has been transmitted for over a thousand years across numerous editions. The most widely circulated version today is the annotated edition by Jin Shengtan, a prominent literary critic of the early Qing dynasty. It is regarded as the foremost Chinese prophetic text, alongside the Shaobing Song and Maqian Ke. However, its authenticity remains hotly debated among scholars — some believe the surviving editions were revised and supplemented over the centuries, with earlier "fulfilled" images possibly added after the fact. This site presents all 60 images with verification analysis for reference.
en.coreQuote: "Endless things could be said, ten thousand upon ten thousand — better to push the back and retire to rest." — Tui Bei Tu, Image 60
en.kp2019: Image 3 — Empress Wu Zetian seizes the throne (Verified)
en.kp2038: Image 21 — Fall of Northern Song / Jingkang Incident (Verified)
en.kp2048: Image 27 — Founding of the Ming Dynasty (Verified)
en.verifySubtitle: Evaluating predictions against reality for expired time points
en.footerNote: Content compiled from public sources, for reference and discussion only. Tui Bei Tu exists in many versions; the most popular is Jin Shengtan's annotated edition, whose authenticity and date of composition remain debated. Some "verified" images may have been added retroactively. Evaluations represent editorial opinions.
